baltic-danish-isles-week.metaDesc: Sail a week from Heiligenhafen across the Fehmarn Belt to Langeland, Aero and Sonderborg. Harbour, mooring and shore tips for all seven days.

1.shortDescription: A short shakedown hop across to Fehmarn, ending at Burgtiefe's modern marina and the long sandy spit of the Suedstrand.
1.description:
Most crews use the first day to settle in rather than rack up miles, and this leg suits that. It is a gentle run of roughly ten miles from the base marina at Heiligenhafen down and around to Burgtiefe on Fehmarn's southern shore. Leave the pontoon by mid-morning, check the reef lines and winch handles on the way over, and you will still tie up with the afternoon ahead of you.

Burgtiefe is the newest and roomiest of Fehmarn's marinas, well sheltered behind the spit. Behind it runs the Suedstrand, a three-kilometre stretch of sand made for a long barefoot walk once the fenders are out. Provision here if you skipped it in Heiligenhafen. The kiosks near the harbour stock the basics, and the shower block is clean enough to send everyone off happy.
thingsToDo: Walk the three-kilometre Suedstrand beach spit
Provision at the Burgtiefe harbour kiosks
Cycle the flat lanes into Burg town
Sundowner on the marina terrace at Burgtiefe
1.mooringTip: Burgtiefe has box berths on finger pontoons rather than a stern-to quay, so ready your bow lines and a midships spring before you enter. It rarely fills in the early season, but arrive before five in July when the Danish-bound fleet stacks up.

2.shortDescription: The route's main open-water crossing over the Fehmarn Belt to Bagenkop, Langeland's salty old fishing port at the island's southern tip.
2.description:
This is the day you cross a proper stretch of open water. The Fehmarn Belt runs about eighteen kilometres wide with twenty to thirty metres under the keel, and the summer wind usually sits somewhere in the west or south-west, which makes for a fair reach up to Langeland. Currents here are light and mostly wind-driven, so watch the shipping lanes more than the tide.

Bagenkop is the real thing: a working fishing harbour that still smells of tar and salt. If a pound-net boat has just landed its catch, buy fish straight off the quay. Climb the ten-metre tower between the old ferry berth and the marina for the sunset, then book a table at Bagenkop Kro, whose fish buffet opens at six. Wednesday evenings bring live music to the harbour stage.
thingsToDo: Buy fresh catch off the Bagenkop quay
Climb the ten-metre harbour view tower
Book the fish buffet at Bagenkop Kro
Catch Wednesday live music on the harbour stage
2.mooringTip: Bagenkop is stern-to or alongside on the marina side; the old fishing basin is deeper but kept for working boats. Take a box berth by the service building, where the free galley and showers are, and pay the harbour dues at the machine before you wander off.

3.shortDescription: Up Langeland's coast and over to Marstal, Aero's shipbuilding 'Skippertown', with wide jetties and a museum full of ships in bottles.
3.description:
A comfortable passage of around fifteen miles, first north along Langeland's west coast, then west across to the eastern end of Aero. With a westerly filling in you may be hard on the wind for the crossing, so a reef kept in early saves fuss later.

Marstal earned its nickname of Skippertown honestly. This was once Denmark's second shipping town, and the small yards along the harbour still patch up wooden hulls. Give an hour to the Marstal Maritime Museum, where the model ships and bottle-ships tell the island's story better than any plaque. The waterfront is lined with long timber jetties, and the town keeps a genuine working feel rather than a polished one. Ice cream by the harbour, then a slow walk through the boatyards, is how most crews spend the evening.
thingsToDo: Tour the Marstal Maritime Museum's bottle-ships
Watch the wooden-hull yards along the harbour
Walk the long timber jetties at dusk
Ice cream on the Marstal waterfront
3.mooringTip: Marstal's marina lies at the north end, past the commercial quay; go stern-to on your own anchor or to a box berth as directed. It shoals toward the inner corners, so favour the outer pontoons if you draw more than two metres.

4.shortDescription: A lazy short hop around Aero to Aeroskobing, a twelfth-century town of pastel houses, cobbled lanes and painted bathing huts.
4.description:
The shortest leg of the week, barely seven miles around the south and west of the island. There is no reason to leave early. Take a swim off the boat, let the crew sleep in, and you can still be moored at Aeroskobing well before lunch.

The town is the reason people keep returning to Aero. Its lanes date to the twelfth century, laid with cobbles and lined with houses painted in soft yellows, ochres and pale blues, doors opening straight onto the street. Walk out to the beach on the north side to see the row of gaily painted bathing huts, then come back for supper; the fish restaurants near the harbour are genuinely good. Evenings here go quiet early, which most crews find is exactly the point.
thingsToDo: Wander the twelfth-century cobbled lanes
Photograph the painted bathing huts on the beach
Eat fresh fish near Aeroskobing harbour
Watch the harbour go quiet at dusk
4.mooringTip: Aeroskobing's harbour is small and popular in July, so it pays to arrive by early afternoon; rafting up two or three deep is normal and expected. Berthing is alongside or stern-to the pontoons, and in high season the harbour office assigns you a spot rather than leaving it first-come.

5.shortDescription: West to the island of Als and into Sonderborg, mooring below a twelfth-century waterside castle a short walk from town.
5.description:
Around twenty miles west today, leaving the Aero group behind and crossing to the island of Als. The approach into Sonderborg runs through the narrow sound under King Christian X's bridge, so call ahead for the opening times and keep an eye on the ferry traffic. Wind tends westerly again, giving a decent sail for most of the way.

Sonderborg is the biggest town on the route and sees more than fourteen thousand visiting yachts a year, so the facilities are excellent. Berth in the south harbour and Sonderborg Castle sits right above you, a red-brick pile from the twelfth century that now holds the regional history museum. The shopping street is two minutes off the quay, the town beach is close by, and across the water Dybbol Mill marks the old battlefield ridge.
thingsToDo: Tour Sonderborg Castle's history museum
Swim at the town beach by the marina
Browse the shopping street off the quay
Look across to Dybbol Mill's ridge
5.mooringTip: Head for the south harbour between the bridge and the castle, rather than Marina Fiskenaes, if you want to be in the middle of town; it is alongside and stern-to, deep enough for most keels. In peak weeks call the harbour master on the way in, as the central berths go first.

6.shortDescription: The long reach back southeast to Bagenkop, a chance to sail hard across open water and settle into Langeland's fishing port again.
6.description:
The homeward run begins with a solid passage of around twenty-two miles back down to Langeland. With the prevailing westerly on the beam this is often the best sail of the week, the boat heeled and moving, Als dropping astern. Time your start to the wind rather than the clock; if it is blowing hard from the west, an earlier departure gets you in before it builds.

Coming back to Bagenkop feels different the second time. You know the harbour now, so there is no scramble for a berth. This is the evening to catch what you missed on the way out: smoked fish off the harbour stalls, or a beer on the quay while the pound-net boats come and go. The sunset from the view tower is worth the climb twice.
thingsToDo: Try smoked fish from the harbour stalls
Climb the view tower for a second sunset
Watch the pound-net boats work the quay
Refuel and top up water before the last leg
6.mooringTip: You will know the layout by now: box berths by the service building, deeper water in the old basin. If the forecast for the final crossing looks fresh, take a berth on the sheltered inner side so you are not fighting the boat off the pontoon in the morning.

7.shortDescription: The final crossing back over the Fehmarn Belt to Heiligenhafen, the longest open-water leg and a fitting last day at sea.
7.description:
The last leg is the longest crossing of the week, roughly twenty-five miles from Bagenkop back across the Fehmarn Belt to the base at Heiligenhafen. Leave by nine to give yourself margin, and check the forecast the night before, since a strong westerly on the nose can turn this into a long slog.

Most of the day is open water, with the low green line of Fehmarn slowly rising ahead. Keep clear of the shipping in the belt and hold your course for the Heiligenhafen approach, which is well marked as you close the coast. Once you are through the outer moles the marina is straightforward to enter. Refuel, flush the heads and hand back a tidy boat; there is usually time for a last plate of fish on the harbour before the drive home.
thingsToDo: Watch Fehmarn rise on the horizon
Refuel and settle up at Heiligenhafen marina
Last harbourside fish plate before departure
Hand back a rinsed, tidy boat
7.mooringTip: Heiligenhafen's marina is large and well marked; follow the buoyed channel in and take your assigned return berth rather than picking your own. Book fuel ahead if you need a full tank for the handover, as the pump can queue on Saturday changeover mornings.

baltic-fyn-circle-14-days.metaDesc: Sail two weeks from Heiligenhafen across the Fehmarn Sound to Ærø, Svendborg, Faaborg and Als, then home through the South Funen archipelago.

1.shortDescription: A gentle shakedown leg out of Heiligenhafen, under the Fehmarn Sound bridge and round to Burgtiefe's big modern marina.
1.description:
Most crews use this first day to shake the boat down rather than chase miles. You clear Heiligenhafen's long breakwater, point west, and pass beneath the Fehmarn Sound bridge that links the island to the mainland. From there it is a short reach around to Burgtiefe on Fehmarn's south coast.

The marina is the newest and roomiest of the island's five, with around 600 berths set behind a distinctive round basin. Fuel, a chandlery and hot showers are all on site, so it is a sensible place to sort gear before the open crossing tomorrow.

Ashore, the Südstrand runs for kilometres of pale sand, popular with kite and windsurfers when the breeze fills in. Grab an early night. The hop to Denmark wants a fresh crew and a settled forecast.
thingsToDo: Walk the long Südstrand kite-surf beach
Top up fuel at Burgtiefe bunker station
Stock the galley in Burg town
Watch windsurfers off Fehmarn's south shore
1.mooringTip: Burgtiefe has space for visitors even in August, so booking is rarely essential, but call ahead on a summer weekend. Box berths take stern-lines to posts, and depths in the basin hold around 3 metres.

2.shortDescription: The proper Baltic crossing north to Denmark, open water all the way to the red-timber fishing harbour of Bagenkop on Langeland.
2.description:
This is the leg that feels like a passage. Once clear of Fehmarn there is little between you and Langeland's southern tip, roughly twenty miles of open Baltic with the coast dropping astern. A southwesterly gives a comfortable reach; anything in the northwest makes it a wetter beat, so we usually wait for the right slot rather than force it.

Bagenkop sits at the bottom of Langeland, a working fishing port where red clapboard sheds line the quay. It is unpretentious and all the better for it. The smokehouse by the harbour sells fish straight off the boats, and the dunes and nature reserve south of town make a good stretch of the legs after a day at the wheel.

First Danish courtesy flag of the trip goes up here.
thingsToDo: Buy smoked fish at the harbour smokehouse
Walk the dunes south of Bagenkop
Spot birds in the coastal reserve
Raise the Danish courtesy flag
2.mooringTip: Bagenkop is a straightforward alongside or box-berth stop with good shelter, but it fills with crews making the same crossing. Arrive by mid-afternoon in July to be sure of a place near the smokehouse end.

3.shortDescription: A short skip across to Ærø and Marstal, once Denmark's second shipping town, still ringed by shipyards and sail lofts.
3.description:
A gentler day after the crossing. You leave Bagenkop and lay a course west across the mouth of the archipelago to Ærø, the long low island that anchors this whole cruising ground. Fifteen miles or so, usually on a beam reach.

Marstal earns its keep. In the age of sail it was Denmark's second port after Copenhagen, and the town still smells faintly of tar and varnish. The Marstal Maritime Museum spreads across several old houses and is worth an afternoon, packed with ship models, figureheads and the story of the schooner fleets built on this very shore. Wander the harbour front, watch a wooden hull being coaxed back to life in one of the yards, then find a table for the evening. The pace here is slow on purpose.
thingsToDo: Tour the Marstal Maritime Museum
Watch wooden boatbuilding in the yards
Walk the long hand-built harbour breakwater
Browse the tar-scented harbour front
3.mooringTip: Marstal's yacht harbour lies just west of the commercial quay; head for the guest pontoons rather than the fishing basin. Depths are good, but the outer berths roll a little in a fresh southerly, so tuck inside if you can.

4.shortDescription: North through the islands into Svendborgsund, Denmark's busiest strait, ending at the lively maritime town of Svendborg.
4.description:
Today you sail into the heart of the archipelago. From Marstal the course runs north, threading between Ærø, Tåsinge and a scatter of smaller islands until the water narrows into Svendborgsund. The strait twists like a river between wooded banks and is reckoned the most sailed stretch in all of Denmark, so keep a good lookout for the little M/S Helge steamer that has puffed up and down here for over a century.

Svendborg itself is a proper working town, the second largest on Funen, with the marina an easy walk from shops and the station. Frederiksøens Fiskebar on the old shipyard island does honest seafood by the water. If you have an hour, cross to Tåsinge for Valdemars Slot, a moated manor dating to the 1640s.
thingsToDo: Eat seafood at Frederiksøens Fiskebar
Visit Valdemars Slot on Tåsinge
Watch the M/S Helge steamer pass
Wander Svendborg's old harbour lanes
4.mooringTip: Svendborg has several basins; the guest harbour on Frederiksø or the marina east of the bridge both work. It is a busy hub, so aim to be moored by late afternoon, and mind the current running through the sund.

5.shortDescription: A classic archipelago day west to Faaborg, past Lyø and Avernakø, finishing under the town's old bell tower.
5.description:
Some skippers call this the prettiest leg of the fortnight, and it is hard to argue. Leaving Svendborgsund you bear west across the open water of the archipelago, with the low green humps of Lyø and Avernakø off to port. Both are worth a detour if the day is young; Lyø's tiny harbour and duck-pond village is a Danish postcard.

Faaborg waits on the Funen shore. Cobbled streets climb from the quay to the medieval Klokketårnet, the old bell tower, and the climb up its steps pays off with roofs and water spread below. The Faaborg Museum holds the best collection of the Funen Painters anywhere. Down by the marina, cafés and fish stalls line the water, and the ice cream at the harbour is something of an institution.
thingsToDo: Climb Faaborg's Klokketårnet bell tower
See the Funen Painters at Faaborg Museum
Detour to Lyø's harbour village
Try the harbour-front ice cream
5.mooringTip: Faaborg's guest harbour sits right below the old town, handy for everything ashore. It can crowd in high summer; if the inner berths are full, the newer pontoons to the east have depth and rarely fill.

6.shortDescription: South again to Ærøskøbing, the best-preserved 18th-century town in Denmark, its cobbled lanes lined with candy-coloured houses.
6.description:
Back south now, recrossing the archipelago to Ærø's north coast and the little harbour of Ærøskøbing. It is a modest sail, fifteen miles give or take, which leaves the afternoon free for the town, and this is one you will remember.

Ærøskøbing has barely changed in two hundred years. Streets of hollyhock-fringed houses painted in mustard, ox-blood and pale green run down to the water, and the whole place is protected as one of the finest 18th-century towns in Scandinavia. Come evening, after the day-trip ferry has gone, the cobbles empty and the light turns gold. Walk out to the row of brightly painted bathing huts on the beach, buy an ice at the old grocer, and let the crew off the leash for a couple of hours ashore.
thingsToDo: Stroll Ærøskøbing's 18th-century cobbled streets
Photograph the painted beach bathing huts
Visit the old-fashioned harbour grocer
Catch the golden evening light ashore
6.mooringTip: Ærøskøbing's small harbour is deservedly popular, so arrive before the afternoon rush or after the last ferry to find room. Rafting up is normal here; take lines early and be ready to accept a neighbour alongside.

7.shortDescription: A longer westward passage to Dyvig, a landlocked natural harbour on Als and one of the loveliest anchorages in Denmark.
7.description:
The longest leg of the cruise takes you off Ærø and out toward Als, the big island guarding the entrance to the Little Belt. Reckon on the better part of a day, twenty-five miles or so, with the fetch building a little in the more open water halfway across.

The reward is Dyvig. A narrow, well-marked entrance opens into an almost completely enclosed pool, ringed by beech woods and farmland, with barely a house in sight. Two small marinas share the cove, the member-run Dyvig Bådelaug and the newer Dyvig Bro, both quiet and friendly. Once the lines are on, it is the sort of spot where you swim off the transom, light the grill and watch the sun drop behind the trees. Bring provisions; there is little ashore beyond the water's edge.
thingsToDo: Swim in Dyvig's sheltered pool
Walk the beech woods around the cove
Grill dinner off the transom
Sundowner from the Dyvig Bro pontoon
7.mooringTip: Dyvig's entrance is narrow but well buoyed; take it slowly and keep to the marked channel. Both marinas hold good depth, and being fully enclosed the pool stays calm in almost any wind, which makes it a reliable bolt-hole.

8.shortDescription: A relaxed run down the coast of Als and through the Alssund to Sønderborg, its castle and marina crowning the strait.
8.description:
An easier day. You slip out of Dyvig and turn south down the Als shore, then into the Alssund, the narrow channel that separates the island from the Jutland mainland. The water is sheltered and the sailing gentle, a good chance to let someone new take the helm.

Sønderborg opens up at the southern end, its marina voted Denmark's harbour of the year in 2024 and drawing more than fourteen thousand visiting crews a season. The whitewashed Sønderborg Slot stands right on the waterfront, part fortress, part museum, telling the tangled Danish-German history of this borderland. The town has good restaurants, a proper supermarket for a mid-cruise restock, and the Christian X bridge lifts on the hour if you want to carry on north. Most crews are happy just to stay put.
thingsToDo: Tour Sønderborg Slot on the waterfront
Restock at the town supermarket
Watch the Christian X bridge lift
Dine along the Sønderborg quay
8.mooringTip: Sønderborg's award-winning marina sits just south of the road bridge on the town side; it is large and well run but busy, so book ahead in peak weeks. Watch the tidal set through the Alssund as you approach the berths.

9.shortDescription: The turn for home begins, a longer reach back east across the belt to the painted lanes of Ærøskøbing.
9.description:
The cruise turns for home, though home is still a comfortable week away. From Sønderborg you retrace part of the outbound track, back through the Alssund and east across the open water toward Ærø. It is another full leg of around twenty-five miles, and with the prevailing southwesterly behind you it can be a fast, satisfying run.

Ærøskøbing makes such a good harbour that a second night hardly feels like repetition. Anything you missed the first time waits: the maritime and bottle-ship museums, a longer walk out along the shore, a proper sit-down dinner ashore. If the crew fancies a change, the anchorage in the bay just east of the town gives a quiet night on the hook with the harbour lights off the bow.
thingsToDo: See the Ærø bottle-ship collection
Anchor in the bay east of town
Revisit a favourite Ærøskøbing café
Walk the shoreline path at dusk
9.mooringTip: If Ærøskøbing's harbour is packed on your return, the bay just east offers good holding in sand in two to four metres, sheltered from anything with west in it. Set plenty of scope and check the night's forecast first.

10.shortDescription: The shortest leg of the trip, a lazy morning coast-hop along Ærø's shore from Ærøskøbing back to Marstal.
10.description:
Barely a passage at all, this one. Ærøskøbing to Marstal is a handful of miles along the island's southern edge, so there is no need to rush breakfast. Wait for the wind to fill, then make an easy sail of it around the eastern tip of Ærø, close enough inshore to pick out farms and the odd church spire.

The short day leaves plenty of time in Marstal, which rewards a second look. If the museum kept you busy last time, this visit is for the town itself: the old sea captains' houses, the boatyards still turning out wooden hulls, and the long hand-built breakwater you can walk to the very end. There are good places to eat by the harbour, and a chandlery if the boat needs anything before the crossing back to Germany.
thingsToDo: Walk to the end of Marstal breakwater
Admire the old sea captains' houses
Restock at the Marstal chandlery
Sail close inshore past Ærø's spires
10.mooringTip: Same guest pontoons as before, west of the commercial harbour. With such a short day you can arrive early and pick a sheltered inside berth ahead of the crews still making their way up from Germany.

11.shortDescription: Back across open water to Bagenkop on Langeland, staging for the German crossing and a last quiet Danish fishing harbour.
11.description:
Now you start setting up the return. From Marstal it is a straightforward reach back east to Bagenkop, retracing the water you crossed on day three, roughly fifteen miles with the archipelago falling away behind. Take a long last look; the Danish islands are astern for good after tonight.

Bagenkop is the right place to stage. It is the closest Danish harbour to Fehmarn, which shortens tomorrow's open crossing, and it is a calm, honest little port to spend a final night in Denmark. Top up fuel and water, buy a last round of smoked fish from the harbour smokehouse, and check the forecast carefully. The Fehmarn leg wants settled conditions. If the wind looks wrong, this is a comfortable place to sit a day and wait it out.
thingsToDo: Buy a last round of smoked fish
Top up fuel and water tanks
Walk Bagenkop's beach one more time
Study the forecast for the crossing
11.mooringTip: Bagenkop is easy alongside or in a box berth with solid shelter. Take a berth near the fuel dock so you can fill up and slip early; the German crossing is best begun in the morning while the wind is still light.

12.shortDescription: The crossing back to Germany, open Baltic water from Langeland's tip to Fehmarn and the familiar round basin at Burgtiefe.
12.description:
The return crossing, and the one open-water leg left in the trip. From Bagenkop you point south into the same twenty-odd miles of Baltic you sailed on the way up, only in reverse, with Germany slowly lifting over the bow. A northerly makes it a gift of a downwind run; a hard southwesterly makes it a slog, which is why the forecast decided whether you left this morning.

Fehmarn's south coast and the round harbour at Burgtiefe come as a welcome sight after a day offshore. Clear back in, tie up in the roomy basin, and mark the moment: the hard sailing of the fortnight is behind you. The Südstrand is a short walk for a swim to rinse off the salt, and the harbour restaurants are a fitting place for a proper crew dinner.
thingsToDo: Swim off the Südstrand to rinse salt
Toast the crossing at a harbour restaurant
Refuel at the Burgtiefe bunker station
Watch surfers off Fehmarn's south shore
12.mooringTip: Burgtiefe's 600-berth basin almost always has room, and after a long crossing it is reassuring to know a place waits. Aim for a leeward pontoon and use the stern posts; the round basin can funnel a fresh breeze across the water.

13.shortDescription: A short, easy leg back toward the mainland, under the Fehmarn Sound bridge to the sheltered marina at Grossenbrode.
13.description:
An undemanding day to unwind the last of the miles. You leave Burgtiefe, work back along Fehmarn's coast and pass once more beneath the Fehmarn Sound bridge, then slip across to Grossenbrode on the mainland side of the sound. Ten miles or so, an afternoon's work at most.

Grossenbrode sits among the largest cluster of charter yachts on the German Baltic, so the marina is well set up for crews winding down. It is quieter than Heiligenhafen and pleasant for a last night afloat, with a long sandy spit for a final swim and a walk. Give the boat a tidy while there is daylight, sort the gear you will hand back tomorrow, and enjoy an unhurried evening. The base is only a short hop away in the morning.
thingsToDo: Swim off the Grossenbrode sandy spit
Take a last walk along the shore
Tidy and de-rig the boat early
Enjoy a quiet final evening afloat
13.mooringTip: Grossenbrode's marina is calm and rarely full late in a charter week, so a berth is easy. Pick one with simple morning access to the sound; the run back to Heiligenhafen is short and you will want a clean getaway.

14.shortDescription: The last short hop back across to Heiligenhafen, an easy morning sail to close the circle at the home marina.
14.description:
The final leg, and a gentle one to finish on. From Grossenbrode it is a short reach back to Heiligenhafen, only a handful of miles across the mouth of the bay, so there is no pressure to leave at dawn. Most crews take a leisurely breakfast, catch the morning breeze and sail the last stretch in an hour or two.

Heiligenhafen's big marina closes the loop where it started a fortnight ago. Time your arrival to the base's handback slot, top up fuel on the way in, and bring her alongside for the last time. Two weeks and something over a hundred and fifty miles are behind you, with a proper mix of open crossings and sheltered island sailing. Settle the paperwork, unload, and find a harbour table for a last cold beer before the drive home.
thingsToDo: Refuel before the Heiligenhafen handback
Toast the trip at a harbour café
Browse Heiligenhafen's waterfront promenade
Settle the charter paperwork at base
14.mooringTip: Confirm your handback berth and fuelling slot with the base before you leave Grossenbrode. Heiligenhafen's guest and charter pontoons are well marked; approach dead slow, as the marina is large and often busy with departing and returning crews.

baltic-germany.metaTitle: German Baltic & Danish Isles Sailing Itineraries | Boat4You
baltic-germany.metaDesc: Day-by-day charter routes from Heiligenhafen across the German Baltic to Ærø, Marstal and the South Funen islands. 7- and 14-day sailing plans.
baltic-germany.title: Sail Germany's Baltic Into the Danish Isles
baltic-germany.description: Short hops and forgiving water make this one of the easiest cruising grounds in northern Europe. From Heiligenhafen and the Fehmarn Sound bases you cross to Langeland in an afternoon, then string together the South Funen islands where legs rarely top 20 miles and shelter is never far. Summer winds sit in the Force 3-4 range most days, with the odd blow rolling through, and the season runs from mid-May to late September. Ærøskøbing rewards an early arrival; the cobbled 18th-century town fills its small harbour fast, so tie up before the afternoon ferry crowd. Marstal, just around the island, keeps its shipping past alive in a maritime museum stacked with model boats. Svendborg opens the door to Skarø and Drejø for lazy anchoring days. Insider note — skip the busy Sønderborg town quay on the way back and slip into Hørup Hav, four miles east, for a quiet last night.
